title: SEGMENT 3 — The liver's metabolic kitchen: CYP450 enzymes

lines.text: SEGMENT 3 — The liver's metabolic kitchen: CYP450 enzymes
Now let's go into the engine room.

Because if I told you there was one family of enzymes in your body that, more than any other, is responsible for how you handle prescription drugs — one family that determines whether a drug works, whether it builds up to toxic levels, whether it gets activated or destroyed before it can do its job — it would be this family.
The cytochrome P450 enzymes.
CYP450, often just written CYP.
Pronounced "sip" by people in the field, although you'll hear "see-why-pee" too.
Here's the analogy I want you to carry.
Think of your liver as a giant metabolic kitchen.
Every drug you swallow gets absorbed from your gut, goes into your bloodstream, and the very first stop is the liver — through what's called the hepatic portal vein.
Before the drug ever reaches the rest of your body, it has to pass through this kitchen.
And in this kitchen are dozens of chefs, each with a slightly different specialty.
Some chefs are slicing, some are chopping, some are turning ingredients into something edible, some are turning food scraps into garbage that the kidneys can flush.
Each chef is an enzyme.
And the CYP450 family is the dominant set of chefs in this kitchen.
Roughly seventy-five percent of all prescription drugs are metabolized, in some way, by CYP450 enzymes.
Three-quarters.
If you pulled up a random drug from a random pharmacy and asked "which enzyme handles this in the liver?
" — three times out of four, the answer is a CYP.
Why is one enzyme family so dominant?
Because of what they do mechanistically.
CYP450 enzymes use an iron-containing heme group to insert an oxygen atom into a target molecule.
It sounds simple, but adding a single oxygen has profound consequences.
It usually makes the molecule more water-soluble, which is the first step toward getting it out of your body through the urine.
It often inactivates a drug's pharmacological activity.
Sometimes — this is critical — it activates a drug, taking a chemically inert "prodrug" and converting it into the form that actually does the therapeutic work.
The same oxygen insertion can wear many hats.
There are dozens of CYP enzymes encoded in your genome.
About fifty-seven in humans, organized into families.
But for drug metabolism, a small handful do most of the work.
Let me introduce you to the cast.
CYP3A4.
The biggest workhorse.
CYP3A4 metabolizes somewhere between thirty and fifty percent of all prescription drugs, depending on whose count you trust.
It's promiscuous — it'll take on a huge variety of substrates.
Statins, many chemotherapies, immunosuppressants like cyclosporine and tacrolimus, many benzodiazepines, certain HIV drugs.
CYP3A4 is the chef on the line who can cook anything, and so most of the orders come through him.

Genetic variation in CYP3A4 itself is relatively modest in its impact — it's not the gene that explains most of the variability — but the gene is heavily influenced by drug-drug interactions, which is why CYP3A4 inhibitors and inducers are such a big deal in clinical pharmacology.
CYP2D6.
Probably the most genetically variable CYP, and the one that's been studied most in pharmacogenomics.

CYP2D6 metabolizes about twenty-five percent of prescription drugs, including many psychiatric medications — SSRIs like fluoxetine and paroxetine, tricyclic antidepressants, certain antipsychotics — and importantly, the opioid codeine.
We'll spend an entire segment on what goes wrong when CYP2D6 doesn't function normally.
CYP2C9.
Major handler of the blood thinner warfarin, the diabetes drug glipizide, the painkiller ibuprofen, and several others.
Variants in CYP2C9 are the main genetic reason warfarin dosing varies so dramatically.
CYP2C19.
Activates the prodrug clopidogrel — the blockbuster antiplatelet drug used after stents — and metabolizes proton pump inhibitors like omeprazole and several antidepressants.
We'll come back to clopidogrel; the CYP2C19 story is one of pharmacogenomics' clearest cases.
CYP2B6.
Smaller but increasingly important.
It metabolizes the HIV drug efavirenz, the smoking-cessation drug bupropion, and certain anesthetics.
Variants in CYP2B6 are particularly relevant in HIV pharmacology, especially in African populations where certain variants are common.
That's your starting cast.
CYP3A4, CYP2D6, CYP2C9, CYP2C19, CYP2B6.
Memorize these five names.
If you remember nothing else specific about the CYP family, remember these five, and you'll be able to follow virtually any pharmacogenomics discussion.
Now, one more thing about the kitchen analogy that I want to drive home, because it sets up everything that comes next.
Different people have, genetically, different chefs.
Some people have a fully staffed kitchen with all the chefs working at full speed.
Some people have a chef who works incredibly slowly — they're competent, they just take forever.
Some people have a chef who's missing entirely.
Some people have an extra chef on the line, working twice as fast as normal.
And the same dish — the same drug, in the same kitchen — comes out completely differently depending on the staffing.
That's pharmacogenomics.
The variation isn't in the recipe.
It isn't in the ingredients.
It's in the staffing of the kitchen.
